articles.title: Solar Basics for Homeowners: How Grid-Tied Systems Work
articles.summary: An introductory guide to how grid-tied residential solar systems generate power, interact with your utility, and reduce your bill.
articles.body:
Grid-tied solar systems are the most common choice for homeowners because they connect directly to your existing utility service. During the day, your panels generate DC power, which is converted to usable AC power by an inverter. Your home uses this power first. Any surplus flows back to the grid, offsetting your consumption when the sun isn’t shining.

Key components include solar panels, an inverter, mounting hardware, and optional monitoring. Because the grid acts as your backup, you may not need batteries, which keeps upfront costs lower. However, this also means that when the grid goes down, most grid-tied systems shut off for safety, unless they are designed with backup capability.

Typical residential systems range from 4–10 kW, depending on your roof space and energy usage. A 5 kW system can often cover a significant share of the usage for a modest single-family home. Your installer will help you size the system based on your historical electricity bills and future plans, like adding an EV.

Understanding how your solar system interacts with your utility’s rate structure, including time-of-use pricing and net metering or its alternatives, is critical to accurately estimating savings.

articles.title: Net Metering in 2026: Policy Updates Homeowners Need to Know
articles.summary: A 2026 overview of net metering changes, successor tariffs, and what they mean for new solar customers.
articles.body:
Net metering has been one of the most important policies driving residential solar adoption. In 2026, many states are revising their programs, shifting toward "net billing" or time-of-export credits rather than simple one-for-one retail credit.

Under classic net metering, every excess kilowatt-hour (kWh) you send to the grid earns you a full retail credit that offsets your usage later. Newer programs may credit exports at a lower rate, or vary the credit by time of day. This can reduce the value of daytime exports but may still offer strong savings, especially when combined with smart inverter settings and optional battery storage.

Key 2026 trends include:
- Transition to export credit rates aligned with grid value.
- Greater incentives for pairing solar with batteries.
- Time-of-use rate structures that reward evening self-consumption.

If you’re planning a system this year, ask your installer which tariff you’ll be placed on, how long your rate is guaranteed, and whether any grandfathering protections apply. A well-designed 5–8 kW system can still deliver attractive payback periods even under new net billing structures.

articles.title: Net Metering vs. Net Billing: What’s the Difference?
articles.summary: Compare net metering and net billing programs to understand which applies to your solar project and how it affects your savings.
articles.body:
As utilities modernize their rate structures, many are moving from traditional net metering to net billing, but the terminology can be confusing. With net metering, excess solar generation is credited at the full retail rate. With net billing, exports are usually credited at a separate, often lower rate than the power you buy.

For example, you might pay $0.25/kWh for electricity you use from the grid but receive only $0.10–$0.15/kWh for excess solar you send back. Under net billing, designing your system to maximize self-consumption becomes more important. Batteries, load shifting (like running appliances during the day), and smart controls play a bigger role in your economics.

When comparing quotes, ask:
- Is my system eligible for net metering or net billing?
- What is the export credit rate, and does it change over time?
- How do time-of-use periods impact my credits?

Understanding these differences can significantly change the optimal system size, whether you choose a 5 kW starter system or a larger 10+ kW array.

articles.title: Net Metering FAQ: Credits, Rollovers, and Annual True-Ups
articles.summary: Answers to the most common questions about how net metering credits work on your utility bill.
articles.body:
New solar customers often have questions about how net metering credits appear on their bill. Under a typical program, any month when your system exports more energy than you use, you’ll accumulate credits measured in kilowatt-hours or dollars. These credits roll forward to offset future usage.

Many utilities perform an annual "true-up" where they reconcile your total exports and imports for the year. If you have a net surplus, you may be paid an avoided-cost rate for the remaining credits, which is usually lower than the retail rate. Because of this, oversizing a system beyond your annual usage is rarely economical.

Key concepts include:
- Monthly vs. annual netting.
- Whether credits rollover indefinitely or expire.
- How rate changes affect existing customers.

If you’re targeting a 5–8 kW system, your installer will typically aim to offset 80–100% of your annual usage, depending on your goals, roof space, and the specifics of your net metering policy.

articles.title: How Big Should a Solar System Be for a 3-Bedroom Home?
articles.summary: Use your monthly bill and home size to estimate the ideal kW size for a 3-bedroom house.
articles.body:
The ideal solar system size depends more on your energy usage than your square footage, but many 3-bedroom homes fall into a predictable range. A typical 3-bedroom home with a $150 monthly electric bill may need a 4–6 kW system, depending on climate and roof orientation.

To estimate system size, you can:
1. Collect 12 months of electricity bills.
2. Calculate your annual kWh usage.
3. Divide by your site’s expected annual production per kW (often 1,300–1,700 kWh/kW).

Our solar calculator automates these steps and lets you experiment with different system sizes and financing options. For many households, a 5 kW kit is a sweet spot: large enough to significantly reduce the bill, but compact enough to fit on most standard roofs.

Once you have a target size, compare equipment options, including inverters and batteries, and review how different plan terms affect your monthly payment.

articles.title: Is a 10+ kWh Solar Battery Right for Your Home?
articles.summary: Learn when it makes sense to add a 10–15 kWh battery to your solar system and how it impacts backup power and bill savings.
articles.body:
Home batteries in the 10–15 kWh range can provide several hours of backup power for critical loads or even whole-home backup for smaller residences. Paired with a 5–8 kW solar system, a 10+ kWh battery can store midday excess generation for use during evening peaks or outages.

Key considerations include:
- Your utility’s rate structure and export credit policy.
- How often you experience outages.
- Whether you plan to add EV charging or electrify heating.

If your utility has shifted away from full retail net metering toward lower export credits, batteries can help you self-consume more of your solar production. However, they also add cost. Many customers find the best value when they combine a right-sized battery with time-of-use optimization, rather than maximizing backup duration alone.

When shopping, compare usable capacity (kWh), power output (kW), cycle life, and warranty, as well as system compatibility with your inverter and 5 kW solar kit.

articles.title: How to Compare Inverter Warranties for 5 kW Systems
articles.summary: Understand warranty terms, coverage, and extension options when choosing a 5 kW inverter.
articles.body:
The inverter is the workhorse of your solar system, converting DC power from the panels into AC power for your home. Because it has electronic components and may handle rapid power fluctuations, it typically has a shorter warranty than solar panels.

When comparing 5 kW inverters under $2,000, look beyond the price tag to the warranty details. Standard warranties range from 5–12 years, with options to extend up to 20–25 years for an additional fee. Pay attention to what’s covered: parts, labor, shipping, and whether on-site service is included.

Also consider:
- Whether the warranty is pro-rated.
- How replacements are handled.
- Required internet connectivity or monitoring.

Choosing the inverter with the longer, more comprehensive warranty can reduce long-term risk and may justify a slightly higher upfront cost, especially for systems expected to operate 25+ years.

articles.title: Do You Need a Solar Maintenance Plan?
articles.summary: Explore what’s included in residential and commercial solar maintenance plans and when they make sense.
articles.body:
Solar systems are relatively low-maintenance, but they are not no-maintenance. Periodic inspections, cleaning, and performance checks can help catch issues early and keep your array operating at peak efficiency.

Residential maintenance plans typically include an annual site inspection, basic electrical checks, and performance reporting. Commercial plans may offer more frequent visits, thermographic inspections, and detailed reporting to meet internal or regulatory requirements.

A 3-year residential maintenance plan can be a good fit for homeowners who prefer predictable costs and professional oversight. For commercial systems, especially 50 kW and larger, structured maintenance is often required to maintain financing or warranty conditions.

When reviewing plan options, compare visit frequency, included services, response times, and whether auto-renew is available to lock in pricing and coverage.

articles.title: Understanding Solar Financing: Loans, Leases, and PPAs
articles.summary: Compare common solar financing structures and how they affect monthly payments and long-term savings.
articles.body:
Solar financing has evolved far beyond simple cash purchases. Today, homeowners and businesses can choose from secured and unsecured loans, leases, and power purchase agreements (PPAs). Each option has different implications for ownership, incentives, and monthly payments.

Loans typically allow you to own the system, claim tax credits, and pay a fixed monthly amount over 10–25 years. Leases and PPAs often involve little or no money down but may offer lower long-term savings because a third party owns the system.

When using our solar calculator, you can compare plans by:
- Monthly payment.
- Total paid over the term.
- Estimated savings vs. your current bill.

If your goal is the lowest monthly payment under a specific budget—for example, under $120 per month—you may favor longer-term loans with lower interest rates. Always consider whether there are prepayment penalties and how rate changes could affect variable products.

articles.title: Residential vs. Commercial Solar: Key Differences in Design and Incentives
articles.summary: Learn how system size, rate structures, and incentives differ between home and business solar projects.
articles.body:
Residential and commercial solar systems share core components but differ in scale, design priorities, and financial structures. Residential systems are typically 4–15 kW, while commercial rooftop or carport arrays can range from 30 kW to several megawatts.

Commercial customers often face demand charges and complex rate schedules. A 50 kW retail system, for example, may be designed not just to offset kWh usage but also to shave peak demand and support critical loads with batteries. Incentives can differ too, with additional depreciation benefits and custom grants for businesses.

The quote process for commercial projects tends to be more detailed, involving load profiles, building plans, and sometimes utility interconnection studies. When requesting a quote, be clear about your business type, operating hours, and whether you’re interested in battery storage options to support resilience or demand management.

articles.title: Roof-Mounted Only: What to Consider If You Don’t Want Ground Mounts
articles.summary: If you prefer roof-mounted panels only, here’s how that affects design, aesthetics, and system size.
articles.body:
Many homeowners prefer the clean look of roof-mounted solar and want to avoid ground-mounted structures. While roof-only designs are common, there are a few considerations to keep in mind.

First, roof space and orientation limit how many panels you can install. A 6–8 kW system often needs 300–400 square feet of usable, unshaded roof area, depending on panel wattage. Chimneys, vents, and skylights can further constrain layout.

Second, tilt and azimuth (direction) affect production. South-facing roofs in the northern hemisphere typically yield the best output, but east-west arrays can also perform well if the system is sized accordingly.

If you specify roof-mounted only in your consultation, your designer will explore panel placement options, structural loading, and aesthetic layouts that work with your roof’s architecture while meeting your energy goals.

articles.title: How to Track Your Solar Equipment Order and Shipping Status
articles.summary: Step-by-step instructions for using the online order tracking tool to monitor shipments.
articles.body:
Once you place an order for solar kits, inverters, or batteries, you can track its progress using our online order tracking portal. You’ll need your order number and the ZIP code for the shipping address.

After entering this information, you’ll see a summary that includes order status, shipment details, and estimated delivery dates. From there, you can open the order details view, which provides a timeline of key milestones such as order confirmation, warehouse processing, carrier pickup, and delivery.

A print-friendly version of the shipment status page is available if you need records for your installer or internal documentation. If the status doesn’t update for several days, contact support with your order number for assistance.

articles.title: Calculating the ROI of a 5 kW Solar Kit in 2024
articles.summary: Estimate payback period and return on investment for a typical 5 kW grid-tied solar kit.
articles.body:
A 5 kW solar kit is one of the most popular sizes for homeowners. To calculate its ROI, you’ll want to compare upfront cost, available incentives, and projected bill savings over time.

Start with the installed cost of your kit, including hardware, permits, and labor. Subtract any tax credits or rebates to find your net cost. Then, estimate annual production based on your location—often 6,500–8,500 kWh per year for a 5 kW system.

Next, apply your current electricity rate and expected rate escalation to estimate savings over the system’s lifetime. Under favorable net metering policies, payback periods of 6–9 years are common. Under net billing, payback may extend, but can be improved with smart load management or battery storage.

Include maintenance, inverter replacement, and financing costs in your analysis to get a realistic picture of long-term returns.

articles.title: Battery Storage Options for 50 kW Commercial Solar Systems
articles.summary: Explore how commercial customers pair 50 kW solar arrays with batteries for resilience and demand management.
articles.body:
Commercial solar systems around 50 kW are common for retail and small office buildings. When paired with batteries, these systems can do more than offset energy use—they can support backup power and reduce costly demand charges.

Battery options range from modular systems that start around 30–40 kWh of capacity to larger cabinets or containerized solutions. Key design questions include which loads need backup, acceptable outage duration, and how often demand peaks occur.

When requesting a commercial quote, specify that you want battery storage options included. Provide details about your operating hours, critical circuits, and whether you want full-building or partial backup. This information helps designers size both the solar array and storage system effectively.

articles.title: Using Your Reading List to Organize Solar Research
articles.summary: Save and organize Learning Center articles so you can revisit important solar topics later.
articles.body:
Going solar involves a lot of research, from understanding net metering to comparing battery options. Our Reading List feature lets you bookmark articles so you can return to them quickly.

Whenever you’re viewing an article, look for the "Save to Reading List" or bookmark icon. Clicking it adds the article to your personal Reading List page, accessible from the site header or footer. From there, you can sort saved items, remove ones you’ve finished, and keep notes as you narrow down equipment and financing choices.

Many customers save key guides on rate policies, 5 kW system sizing, and maintenance plans before scheduling a consultation or requesting a quote.
